entries.end_notes:
A Greek form from σαίτης, itself an early loan from the Semitic siˀatu, i.e., Aramaic sˀh, sˀtˀ n.f.
For the relationships among the various terms for field measures see U. Seidel, Altorientalische Forschungen 15(1988): pp. 154ff., and note that Epiph parag. 58ff. specifically notes that the various measures are different in different geographic areas and even when used for different types of soil.

entries.form_section: LJLA emph. sym) thus easily confused with the abs. of symh n.f.
entries.end_notes:
Greek ἄσημος. In Syriac and LJLA generally used as a replacement for Semitic ksp in the sense of the metal as opposed to silver used as "money", although some Syriac scribes were clearly somewhat confused about the difference, and we find frequent variants accordingly.
For the potential example cited here for JBA, see also the note to symh n.f. "treasure".

entries.end_notes: The actual number of different words here is uncertain. The noun formations saḇrā, seḇrā and v.n. sḇārā should probably be considered interchangeable, at least in some dialects or idiolects. It is convenient for lexicographical purposes, however, to distinguish between "opinion" and "hope" even though in Christian and Gnostic thought "hope" and "belief" were tightly woven together.

entries.form_section: MA and older: śgyˀ, śgy; often used adverbially (i.e. without inflection) even in attributive contexts. In LJLA the aleph glide is often replaced by ayin as a spelling convention. In JPA, the adverbial usage normally uses the plural form sgyn. Note that vocalized Cairo Genizah texts of PTA show /e/ in the first syllable.

entries.form_section: OA, OfA, BA, etc. &wm ; D: sayyem. The af'el for peal is normative in CPA, rare in LJLA. The simple T-stems might at least theoretically be divided between OA, OfA, Syr, CPA Gt ˀettəsīm and JLA, LJLA, CPA ˀettəsām, but semantically they are the same and have here been treated as Gt, except in the case of Mandaic, which evidences a clear /a/ vowel after the /t/. BA G passive perfect: &UM
entries.end_notes:
See especially also noun phrases in msm.

For JBA and Mandaic see s.v. swm #2; however, it is almost certain that #1 and #2 are not separate lemmas: It seems that in the standard Jewish Aramaic dialects (and Sam.) this root (in the peal) has become limited to the idea of placing something inside of something else that envelops it, e.g., a shoe or a coffin, having been replaced in its based usage by the pael of שוי. Note also that in LJLA the root occurs only in the Syriac-based targum Proverbs and in calques from the Biblical Aramaic idiom שים טעם, although in JLA, Sam. and LJLA, there are also rare exxs. as Hebraizing variants to the standard שוי.
Note that in Mandaic, too, the meaning of the peal is highly specialized to a) ordaining; b) putting away simta 'treasure', and c) putting on clothing, while the basic meaning has moved to the afel while for other usages the verb tnḥ is used.

The interpretation is based on Onqelos for the passage: דִלמָא יִטַר לַנָא דְבָבוּ יוֹסֵף  
 and as interpreted the same way in LXX.
See also s.v. ˀṣmwmˀ, etc. Greek στόμωμα.

This form replaces stm in the Eastern dialects. The LS2 suggestion for a second root based on the Greek is to be rejected, although the phonetic shift from /t/ to /ṭ/ may have been influenced by the loanword sṭm n.m.
For the grammatical usage see Nestle, ZDMG 32 (1878): 476.

entries.end_notes:
The interpretation is based on the appearance of this word in other rabbinic Hebrew texts related to this passage. See Jastrow and DJBA s.v. רצינתא. The scribe here apparently thought it was a verbal form, however, hence ולא instead of דלא. The etymology, though, (for which compare Levy and Jastrow), remains uncertain.
The standard edition of the Talmud (BB 91b) reads: ת"ר ואכלתם מן התבואה ישן בלא סלמנטון מאי בלא סלמנטון רב נחמן אמר בלא רצינתא ורב ששת אמר בלא שדיפא : the sages taught: you shall eat from the old grain (Lev. 25:22) means without s’. What is "without s’?" PN1 said without רצינתא while PN2 said without blast.
entries.extra_comments: jastrow: salted.  see djba s.v. rcynt)

DJBA has decided to treat the meaning "to strike out or eliminate a text" as G, apparently based on a single JBAgeon. example. The tradition clearly treats this usage as a D, however (see Jastrow), as is logical as well from a grammatical point of view and from the Akkadian parallel there cited, and we have followed that here.

Originally an sT (i.e. Ct) form from ˀmr or the like that has remained in the T stem in Aramaic but moved to full verbal root status in late Babylonian as in the Hebrew cognate שׁמר.
The JBA variant forms with shin treated as a separate verb by DJBA meaning "to direct attention" clearly belong here.
The evidence for the independent existence of a Dt form needs to be seen as uncertain.

entries.end_notes: LowFL 2:200. From Akk. šam terinnu, "coniferous plant" or $aman terinnus "coniferous ointment". Low's identification with "dragon's blood" (see here) makes little sense now, not only because of the Akkadian etymology but because the palm from which such "blood" is extracted is not a western Asiatic one. Rather compare with the widespread medicinal use of the odiferous resins of various trees.

The masc. plural form is generally used for the name of the crop and is thus the most commonly attested. Flora 1:713+.
We have ascribed the first example ofšˁryn at Fakh 19 to šˁr n.m. in the sense of "measure" rather than to this word. See DNWSI. The many other examples of this spelling in Old Aramaic economic texts are probably all this word, however.

entries.end_notes:
Here we encounter yet another example of the idiocy of the lexicography of LS2. This word does not mean storm. It is rather the word ܥܪܡܘܬܐ that provides the semantics of a forceful storm while stwˀ simply tells us it is a winter one.
The example in the "Bauer-Meissner papyrus" has not been generally recognized since it appears to have prefixed "d_" after the same word spelled "zy"; but the latter is of course a literary spelling of a form that had almost certainly already shifted to "d" in pronunciation, so we take the expression to be a fixed expression "wintery, late crop".

But surely not the "Alaskan" silver fox!

Müller-Kessler BSOAS 75(2012):21 proposes that this form is always due solely to orthographic confusion between /ṭ/ and /g/ in Mandaic script. It might be a compelling suggestion except for the facts that the parallel noun from that root (i.s. sṭm) appears as sidma in Mandaic and the root appears in JBAmb, where the graphic confusion would not occur. So we think rather that it is a legitimate lexical expansion from sqm vb. Alternatively (or additionally?) one might compare the Greek loans sgmwṭyn n.m. "covering" and/or zgmˀ n.m.pl. "bolt". See Morgenstern and Ford, BSOAS 80, 2 (2017):216f.
